assignment_description:
Color Theory: Expanded Color Wheel
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes, brushes, water, cloth, printed color wheel

PREP: Print on cardstock
∞ Link: Color Wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall all you know about color thus far.
• Name the primary colors that you used in previous levels.

➜ INTRO
Students will make a 12-color color wheel with primary colors: Phthalo Blue, Quinacridone Gold & Quinacridone Red.

➜ PRACTICE
• Do one half of the color wheel for this lesson.
• Students will need to mix fresh secondary colors from their new
primaries. (Mix enough so that you will have plenty to use for the next
lesson to mix your tertiary colors.)
• The color wheel will be referred to throughout the term, so keep it in a handy place.
One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your Art book.
• Have students paint the colors in their proper places. Color placement
on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you make a mistake.
• Refer to the example if you are confused about placement within the
wheel.
∞ Image Link: Example of Expanded Color Wheel

• OCCUPATIONS Make other color wheels by mixing your previous primaries with the new primaries. Anytime you sub one primary for another you will get a completely new rainbow. So if you kept Phthalo Blue and Quinacridone Gold but used Quinacridone Pink you would get different oranges and purples. If you used Prussian Blue and mixed it with Quinacridone Red you would get a different purple, etc. Just be sure to keep your experimental palette separate from your working palette.

Color Theory: Expanded Color Wheel
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes, brushes, water, cloth, color wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall all you know about color thus far.
• Name the primary colors that you used in previous levels.

➜ PRACTICE
• Do the other half of the color wheel for this lesson.
• Students will need to mix fresh tertiary colors from their new primary
and secondary colors.
• The color wheel will be referred to throughout the term so keep it in a handy place.
One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your Art book.
• Have students paint the colors in their proper places. Color placement
on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you make a mistake.
• Refer to the example if you are confused about placement within the
wheel.
∞ Image Link: Example of Expanded Color Wheel

Color Theory: Contrasting Colors
⍞ Materials: black construction paper, white construction paper, 8 different colors of construction paper, glue or glue sticks, scissors

➜ RECAP
• Recall tertiary colors.
• Recall complementary colors.

➜ INTRO
Today, we are learning about contrasting colors. Contrasting colors are colors that cause other colors to pop out.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Contrasting Colors L8, L3

➜ PRACTICE
• Cut one 4” wide strip of black paper as long as your page. Cut a ~2”
square out of each of the colors of construction paper that you have.
Glue the 2” square onto the black construction paper. (Follow the
instructions on the video.)
• Which color pops out the most?
• Do the same thing with the colors on the white side of the paper.
• Which color pops out the most?

➜ DISCUSS
What did you observe about your colors?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for contrasting colors on your nature walk.

• OCCUPATIONS Choose two contrasting colors to use. Do a design with your name using those two colors.

Color Theory: Contrasting Colors
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, pencil

➜ RECAP
Recall contrasting colors.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Contrasting Colors with Watercolor L8, L4

➜ PRACTICE
• Draw eight 4” squares in your Art book. Draw 2” squares in the center
of each of those squares.
• Choose your favorite color and paint in the 4” square, but do not
paint in the 2” square with that same color.
• After the outside square has dried, paint each 2” square a different
color.

➜ DISCUSS
Which pair contrasts the most?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for contrasting colors on your nature walk.

• OCCUPATIONS Continue working on your name design using contrasting colors.

Color Theory: Contrasting Colors
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, fruit all of one color (optional)

➜ RECAP
Recall contrasting colors.

➜ PRACTICE
Paint a still life with fruit that are all the same color; i.e., strawberries, raspberries, and a red apple. You can choose to do this painting from your memory of fruit or to observe fruit in life. Use a contrasting color for the background, such as green or blue.

➜ DISCUSS
What is the effect the contrasting background has on the fruit?

Color Theory: Introduction to Color Charting
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, 12” x 18” white paper, ruler, pencil, separate palette for mixing (i.e., Styrofoam plate, etc.)

➜ NOTE
This lesson will take the term to complete.

➜ RECAP
• Recall the colors that you have been given this year in your palette
(Quinacridone Gold, Quinacridone Red, Phthalo blue, and neutral).
• Recall all 12 colors on your color wheel.

➜ INTRO
You will begin color charting in order to learn the range of colors that you can get from your palette.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Setting Up Your Color Chart L8, L6
∞ Video Link: Color Charting L8, L6

➜ PRACTICE
• You have 12 colors in your color wheel, and so you will need to draw a 12
by 12 chart. You will draw the boxes 1” square.
• It may take you an entire class time to set up your color chart. This is
to be expected.
• Follow the instructions on the video.

➜ NOTE
Students may refer to the sample color chart if they need further guidance.
∞ Link: How to Make a Watercolor Mixing Chart Step-by-Step

➜ DISCUSS
Tell the process that you went through.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for and collect items on your nature walk that are interesting in color.

• OCCUPATIONS You can do this color charting with your old palette of Prussian Blue, Quinacridone Pink, and Hansa yellow. Read the article if you need more help understanding.
∞ Article Link: How to Make a Watercolor Mixing Chart Step-by-Step

⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, color chart

➜ RECAP
• Recall the colors that you have been given this year in your palette

➜ PRACTICE

• Follow the instructions on the video.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell the process that you went through. Share your thoughts with your teacher.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for and collect items on your nature walk that are interesting in their color.

Color Theory: Introduction to Split-Complementary Color Schemes
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, color wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall the twelve colors that you have on your color wheel.
• Recall complementary colors.

➜ INTRO
Split-complementary is a color scheme using one color and two colors that are adjacent to its complementary color. Split-complementary colors provide visual contrast but less tension than complementary colors.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Split-Complements L8 L11

➜ PRACTICE
Do a design of your name using blue-green, Quinacridone Red, and orange.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell the process that you went through. Share your thoughts with your teacher.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for split complements in nature.

• OCCUPATIONS Draw a still life of fruit using split-complements in your Art book with pastels.

Color Theory: Split-Complements
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, color wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall the twelve colors that you have on your color wheel.
• Recall complementary and split-complementary colors.

➜ PRACTICE
Do a different design of your name using the split-complements of Phthalo Blue, red-orange, and yellow-orange.

➜ DISCUSS
Using your color wheel, walk through the process to find colors that are split-complements. Explain the process to your teacher. Find additional split-complements.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for split complements in nature.

• OCCUPATIONS Draw a portrait of a friend using split-complements in your Art book with pastels.

Color Theory: Split-Complements
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, color wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall the twelve colors that you have on your color wheel.
• Recall complementary colors.

➜ PRACTICE
Do one last design of your name using Quinacridone Red, yellow-green, and blue-green.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Display all three of your name designs next to each other. Compare and contrast the color combinations.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for split complements in nature.

• OCCUPATIONS Look for a bush or plant that displays split-complements and draw it in your Art book with pastels.

Color Theory: Introduction to Analogous Colors
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, color wheel

➜ NOTE
Students will need analogous colored flowers to work with for Lesson 15. Analogous colors are three or four colors beside each other on the color wheel; i.e., blue, purple, red, OR green, blue-green, blue, OR Red-Orange, Orange, Yellow-Orange, Yellow. Students are always encouraged to work from life, but if it is not possible, then they can refer to this link:
∞ Image Link: Analogous Colored Flowers in a Vase

➜ RECAP
Recall the twelve colors on your color wheel.

➜ INTRO
Analogous colors are groups of three colors that are next to each other on the color wheel. An analogous color scheme can have a harmonious, monochromatic effect.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Analogous Colors L8 L14

➜ PRACTICE
Do a landscape painting using any three analogous colors that you choose.

➜ DISCUSS
What effect did the analogous colors have on your landscape painting?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for a photo of a landscape that uses the same three colors that you chose.

Color Theory: Analogous Colors
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book, analogous colored flowers, vase (something to put the flowers in)

PREP: Students will need access to analogous colored flowers (such as Zinnias, Carnations, Dahlias, Petunias, Vincas, Impatients) that they can place in a vase for this lesson.

➜ RECAP
Recall the twelve colors on your color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
• Arrange analogous colored flowers in a vase.
• Do an observational painting of this still life.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to the still life. How well did you color match? What did you do well? What could you improve next time?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for analogous colors when walking by the way.

Color Theory: Analogous Colors
⍞ Materials: pastels, erasers, blending stumps, cloth, Art book

➜ RECAP
• Recall the twelve colors on your color wheel.
• Recall analogous colors

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Analogous Colors/ Painting with a Limited Color Palette

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the video tutorial for this lesson.

Color Theory: Analogous Colors
⍞ Materials: pastels, erasers, blending stumps, cloth, 11x14 watercolor or pastel paper

➜ RECAP
• Recall the twelve colors on your color wheel.
• Recall analogous colors.
• Recall the video tutorial you watched in the last lesson.

➜ NOTE
You will have four total lesson times to render your analogous color painting. Take your time and pace yourself well.

➜ PRACTICE
• Choose a reference photo to work from.
• Decide on an analogous color scheme to render your composition in.
• Create swatches of your chosen colors.
• Begin sketching in your composition with pastel.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
What did you do well? What could you improve next time?

Color Theory: Analogous, Complements, and Split- Complements
⍞ Materials: pastels, erasers, blending stumps, cloth, Art book, watercolors, brushes, water

➜ RECAP

• Recall analogous colors.
• Recall complements and split-complements

➜ PRACTICE

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction: Level 8
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Art: Level 8 — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Explain contrasting colors. List six pairs of contrasting colors. (You can be general with hue names, i.e., yellow as opposed to Hansa yellow.)

· Describe fully what stippling is. How can you use it to apply value? How can you use it to mix colors?

assignment_description:
Drawing: Introduction to Hatching
⍞ Materials: Charcoal, Art book, drawing board, erasers, still life pieces, light source

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to create values with charcoal.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.

➜ INTRO
We are going to learn a new technique called hatching. Hatching is used to create tonal or shading effects by drawing closely spaced parallel lines.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Hatching with Charcoal L8 L25

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a value scale with six 3” squares.
• Use the hatching technique to apply value to your scale.
• Set up a still life.
• Draw the still life using hatching to add value to your objects.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
How well did you use the new hatching technique? How could you improve next time?

• OCCUPATIONS Draw fabric using the hatching technique to add value.

Drawing: Hatching
⍞ Materials: Pencil, Art book, drawing board, erasers, still life pieces, light source

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to create values with a pencil.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.
• Recall hatching.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Hatching in Pencil L8 L26

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a value scale with ten 1” squares.
• Use the hatching technique to apply value to your scale.
• Set up a still life.
• Draw the still life using hatching to add value.

• OCCUPATIONS Set up a new still life and use hatching with the pencil to shade and create shadows.

Drawing: Hatching with Pastels
⍞ Materials: Pastel, Art book, drawing board, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to create values with pastel.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.

➜ INTRO
Today, we will learn how to do hatching with pastels.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Hatching in Pastel L8 L27

➜ PRACTICE
• Use three different color pastels to create three different pastel scales.
(Only one color per scale.)
• Set up three value scales with six 2” squares each. Leave at least an
inch of space between the scales.
• Use the hatching technique to apply value to your scales.

➜ DISCUSS
Which medium do you prefer to do hatching in?

Drawing: Cross Hatching Value Scales
⍞ Materials: Charcoal, Art book, drawing board, erasers, and a tree to observe

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to create values with hatching in charcoal.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.

➜ INTRO
Cross hatching is similar to hatching except lines are placed on top of each other going in opposite directions to create shading or value changes.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Cross-Hatching in Charcoal L8 L28

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a value scale with six 3” squares.
• Use the cross-hatching technique to apply value to your scale.
• Draw a tree using cross-hatching to add value to your objects.

Drawing: Cross-hatch Value Scales with Pencil
⍞ Materials: Pencil, Art book, drawing board, erasers, still life pieces, light source

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to create values with hatching in pencil.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.
• Recall cross-hatching.

➜ INTRO
Today, we will learn how to cross-hatch with pencil.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Cross-Hatching in Pencil L8 L29

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a value scale with ten 1” squares.
• Use the cross-hatching technique to apply value to your scale.
• Set up a still life.
• Draw the still life using cross-hatching to add value.

➜ DISCUSS
Compare and contrast shading with hatching and cross-hatching.

• OCCUPATIONS Set up a new still life and use cross-hatching with the pencil to shade and create shadows.

Drawing: Cross Hatch Scales with Pastels
⍞ Materials: Pastel, Art book, drawing board, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to create values with hatching in pastel.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.

➜ INTRO
Today, we will learn how to cross-hatch with pastels.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Cross-Hatching in Pastel L8 L30

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up three value scales with six 2” squares each. Leave at least an
inch of space between the scales.
• Use only one color of pastel per value scale
• Use the cross-hatching technique to apply value to your scales.

➜ DISCUSS
Which medium do you prefer to use for cross-hatching?

Drawing: Introduction to Stippling
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, pencil, drawing board, Art book

➜ WARM UP
Spend three or four minutes at the chalkboard drawing with large arm motions.

➜ RECAP
• Recall line and value.
• Recall proper technique in holding the pencil for art.

➜ INTRO
Stippling is an art technique that uses small dots or specks to add shading or tone.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Stippling in Pencil L8 L31

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a value scale with eight 1” squares.
• Complete the value scale with stippling in pencil.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Do a self-portrait using stippling for adding value. Remember to hold your pencil properly. Continue this for next lesson as well.

Drawing: Stippling
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, pencil, drawing board, Art book

➜ WARM UP
Spend three or four minutes at the chalkboard drawing with large arm motions.

➜ RECAP
• Recall line and design.
• Recall stippling.

➜ PRACTICE
Find an object in your classroom to look at and draw, holding your pencil properly. Try to add shading by stippling. Do not use erasers for mistakes! Use them as drawing tools and for highlights.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to the object. How well did you apply shading and stippling?

• OCCUPATIONS Continue working on your self-portrait.

Drawing: Stippling
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, pencil, drawing board, Art book

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motion.

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall holding your pencil properly.

➜ PRACTICE
• Do a memory drawing of a plant that you have observed recently.
• Use stippling in pencil to add value to your drawing.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Study these value scales and discuss your observations. The “smooth shading” one is tonal value.

• OCCUPATIONS Do a value scale with stippling in ink. A gel type or any free flowing ink pen would work best. Ball point pens are not as satisfying for stippling.

Drawing: Stippling Value Scale
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book

➜ RECAP
• Recall stippling.
• Recall monochromatic and underpainting.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Stippling in Watercolor L8 L34

➜ PRACTICE
• You will practice the stippling technique with your brush and one
color of watercolor.
• Create a value scale with eight 1” squares.
• Fill it in using stippling in watercolor.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Find other mediums that you can practice stippling with.

Memory Drawing: Bird with Stippling
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book

➜ RECAP
• Recall stippling.
• Recall monochromatic and underpainting.

➜ NOTE
Work on this for two lessons. Take your time and pace yourself well.

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing by stippling. Use one color of watercolor and the tip of your brush.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Stippled Bird in Watercolor

➜ PRACTICE
Do a memory brush drawing of a bird that you have recently studied. Use only one color. Use stippling to add value.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

Memory Drawing: Bird with Stippling
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art book

➜ RECAP
• Recall stippling.
• Recall monochromatic and underpainting.

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing by stippling. Use one color of watercolor and the tip of your brush.

➜ PRACTICE
Finish your bird drawing during this lesson.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Do a stippling portrait of a family member using any of your art materials.

Drawing: Introduction to Pointillism
⍞ Materials: Drawing board, pastel, Art book, pink pearl and kneaded erasers, pencil

➜ RECAP
Recall stippling.

➜ NOTE
Work on this for two lessons. Take your time and pace yourself well.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
The painting by Georges Seurat. Notice how the artist used dots of primary colors next to each other. It’s easiest to see around the edge of the painting. This is called pointillism.
∞ Image Link: A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Stippling to Mix Colors in Pastel L8 L37

➜ PRACTICE
• Draw three 3” circles in your Art book.
• Stipple red & yellow in one, blue & red in the other, and blue & yellow
in the last one.

➜ DISCUSS
What happened?

• OCCUPATIONS Practice creating more perspective drawings in your Art book.

Drawing: Introduction to Pointillism
⍞ Materials: Drawing board, pastel, Art book, pink pearl and kneaded erasers, pencil

➜ RECAP
Recall stippling.

➜ NOTE
Work on this for two lessons. Take your time and pace yourself well.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
The painting by Georges Seurat. Notice how the artist used dots of primary colors next to each other. It’s easiest to see around the edge of the painting. This is called pointillism.
∞ Image Link: A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte

➜ PRACTICE
Finish your mixing by stippling in this lesson.

➜ DISCUSS
What happened?

• OCCUPATIONS Draw and shade spheres in your Art book with charcoals.

Drawing: Fruit- Pencil Drawing
⍞ Materials: Pencil, Art book, erasers, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to hold a pencil properly and mass drawing.
• Recall tone.
• Recall perspective drawing from level 4.

➜ PRACTICE
• Choose a fruit. Lay it on a plate. Set it above eye level.
• Draw what you see.
• Shade your drawing with tonal value.
• Apply your knowledge of perspective in your drawing.
• Do your drawing using pencils in your Art book.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Illustrate a famous quote, in your commonplace book, from the period of History that you are studying.
Set up a still life. Use pastels to do a tonal drawing of the still life.

Drawing: Fruit- Charcoal Drawing
⍞ Materials: Charcoal, Art book, drawing board, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall texture and value.

➜ PRACTICE
• Draw the same fruit on a plate above eye level.
• Shade using only cross-hatching for shading.
• Apply your knowledge of drawing textures, shading, and shadows in
your composition.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS Draw another portrait of the same person using pastels or charcoal.

Drawing: Landscapes in Pastel
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art book, drawing board, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall how to draw texture and add tones.

➜ NOTE
Work on this for two lessons. Take your time and pace yourself well.

➜ PRACTICE
• Sit outside or look out a window.
• Using pastels and stippling, draw what you see.
• Apply your knowledge of tones. Don't forget to notice the tonal
differences between objects.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
How well did you apply tone? What did you do well? What could you improve on next time?

• OCCUPATIONS Continue doing more drawings of people in your Art book, whether close ups of heads or figures in action.

Drawing: Landscapes in Pastel
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art book, drawing board, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall how to draw texture and add tones.

➜ NOTE
Work on this for two lessons. Take your time and pace yourself well.

➜ PRACTICE
• Sit outside or look out a window at the same view. Notice the lighting
and what has changed or moved since the last lesson. You may choose
to leave things as they were or to move them if it makes for a better
composition.
• Using pastels and stippling, draw what you see.
• Apply your knowledge of tones. Don't forget to notice the tonal
differences between objects.
• Finish your drawing in this lesson.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
How well did you apply tone? What did you do well? What could you improve on next time?

• OCCUPATIONS Study the clothing and transportation of the time period that you are studying in History. Do drawings of these objects in your Book of Centuries.

Drawing: Human Portrait
⍞ Materials: Pencil, drawing board, Art book, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall proportions of the human body.
• Recall foreshortening.

➜ NOTE
Work on this for two lessons. Take your time and pace yourself well.

➜ PRACTICE
• Draw a full-body portrait of someone that you have recently studied in
history. Be sure to be historically accurate with their clothing, as it helps
the viewer to understand who the person is.
• Apply what you have learned about foreshortening to the human figure.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell the process that you went through. Share your thoughts with your teacher. Look for a reference photo of the person you drew and compare it to your drawing.

Drawing: Human Portrait
⍞ Materials: Pencil, drawing board, Art book, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall proportions of the human body.
• Recall foreshortening.

➜ PRACTICE
• Finish your full body portrait.
• Apply what you have learned about foreshortening to the human figure.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell the process that you went through. Share your thoughts with your teacher. Look for a reference photo of the person you drew and compare it to your drawing.

Drawing: Portrait
⍞ Materials: Charcoal, erasers, Art book, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.
• Recall proportions of the head and bust.

➜ PRACTICE
Do a portrait of someone that you have recently read about in literature. Focus on the head, neck, and shoulders, and apply all of your knowledge about shading and highlights.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
How well did you use shading and highlights? What did you do well? What could you improve next time?

Drawing: Illustrate an Event from History
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art book, drawing board, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, shadows, texture, and tones.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.
• Recall proportions of the head and bust.

➜ PRACTICE
• Illustrate an event that you have recently studied in History.
• Use your pastels and apply all of the knowledge that you have gained
thus far in Art.

➜ DISCUSS
Point out and describe the techniques in your drawing.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction: Level 8
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Art: Level 8 — TERM 2 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Explain cross-hatching. How do you change the value by cross-hatching?

· Explain how to hold your pencil properly when drawing and shading and why it's important to do so.

assignment_description:
Brush Drawing Review: Nine Watercolor Techniques
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, masking fluid

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall nine techniques learned since Level 1 (wash, graduated wash,
lifting, wet on dry, layering, wet on wet, masking, dry on wet, or
dry-brushing)
∞ Video Link: Watercolor Techniques L6 L17 & L8 L49

➜ PRACTICE
Nine watercolor techniques
• Divide your paper into nine equal spaces by drawing thumbnails.
• Label each thumbnail with a technique. (wash, graduated wash,
lifting, wet on dry, layering, wet on wet, masking, dry on wet, or
dry-brushing)
• Apply each technique to the proper thumbnail.

➜ NOTE
Try to do these from memory, but if you cannot remember one, you can always go back and re-watch the tutorial videos from previous levels.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Which techniques look best?

➜ NOTE
Students should clean, dry, and store the brush and leave the palette open to dry after each use.

• OCCUPATIONS Practice and strengthen your brush stroke by beautifully writing out the alphabet in block letters.

Brush Drawing: Wet on Wet Technique and Graduated Wash
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, and any plant for observation.

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall wet on wet and graduated wash.
∞ Video Link: Watercolor Techniques L6, L17, & L8, L49

➜ PRACTICE
Use these two watercolor techniques while doing an observational brush drawing of a plant.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your brush drawing to the plant.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for ways to apply these watercolor techniques when nature journaling.

• OCCUPATIONS Do a watercolor painting to give as a gift. Use several of the watercolor techniques that you have learned.

Brush Drawing: Wash and Dry on Wet Techniques
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, and any landscape for observation

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall wash and wet on dry.
∞ Video Link: Watercolor Techniques L6 L17 & L8 L49

➜ PRACTICE
Use these two watercolor techniques while doing a landscape painting.

Brush Drawing: Animal using Dry-Brushing and Masking Techniques
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, and any animal for observation.

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall dry-brushing and masking.

➜ PRACTICE
Do an observational brush drawing of an animal using dry-brushing and masking techniques.

Brush Drawing: Plant using Wet on Dry and Layering Techniques
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, and any plant for observation

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall wet on dry and layering.

➜ PRACTICE
Do a brush drawing of a plant using wet-on-dry and layering techniques.

Brush Drawing: Illustration using Underpainting and Lifting Techniques
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, Art book

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall underpainting and lifting.

➜ PRACTICE
Illustrate a scene from your recent history lessons. Begin with an underpainting and use the lifting technique.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell about your painting.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for ways to apply these watercolor techniques when nature journaling.

• OCCUPATIONS Continue working on strengthening your brush stroke by beautifully writing out the alphabet in cursive letters.

Brush Drawing: Illustrated Motto, Fable, or Proverbs Project 1
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palette, nice quality paper (watercolor paper or cardstock)

PREP: Students will be creating four different cards with a different motto/fable/proverb on each one, but all four should follow a central theme. They will be given 3 lessons to work on each project. When they are finished with the last lesson on the last day, they will have a set of cards.

➜ WARM UP
Make lines with your brush in all directions on scrap paper.

➜ RECAP
Recall line, design, and pattern.

➜ INTRO
This term, you will illustrate four different mottoes, proverbs, or fables of your choice, all with a central theme. You can choose to do any combination, such as 2 mottoes and 2 proverbs, or one each of mottoes, proverbs, and fables with a second proverb, or all fables. You will be given three lesson times to work on each illustration.

➜ NOTE
These are great pieces to give as gifts, put in your commonplace books, or decorate your room.

➜ PRACTICE
• Choose a theme. Here are some examples that may be used for
inspiration (use discretion).
∞ Image Link: Netherlandish Proverbs
∞ Article Link: U.S. State Mottos
• Begin your first illustrated motto, proverb, or fable. You can choose
to do a border design or an illustration.

• OCCUPATIONS Work on different types of lettering with brush drawing so that you will have a variety to choose from for your term work.

➜ PRACTICE

∞ Video Link: How to Paint Light in Watercolor

➜ PRACTICE

Look for a reference photo with a strong light source in it that you will paint for the next few lessons.
